summary:
Tell Sotto is a small Neolithic site in northern Iraq, near the town of Telafar in the Sinjar district, dated to approximately 6500 to 6000 BCE and identified as an early component of the cultural sequence preceding the [[event:1403|Hassuna culture]] in the northern Mesopotamian farming zone. Excavated by Soviet archaeologists during the 1970s as part of broader collaborative research in northern Mesopotamia, the site contributes to understanding the pre-Hassuna Neolithic of this region.

The pottery from Tell Sotto, known as Sotto ware, represents a transitional or early phase of ceramic production in northern Mesopotamia, preceding the better-defined Hassuna tradition. The site's material culture helps archaeologists identify the local trajectory of ceramic development in this part of the Fertile Crescent. The community subsisted on farming and herding, consistent with the broader Neolithic agricultural complex of the region.

Tell Sotto is significant as a small but informative data point in the broader picture of early Neolithic settlement across the northern Mesopotamian plains and foothills. Sites like Tell Sotto, [[event:1473|Umm Dabaghiyah]], and early Hassuna communities together document the variety of community types, economic strategies, and material culture traditions that characterized the Neolithic of this region before the emergence of the more clearly defined cultural horizons of the later sixth millennium BCE.
